burial.plot_notes: Jackson Cemetery, Tunkhannock, Wyoming County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Miner's History of Wyoming.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army surgeon; Old Forge, PA namesake

cross_connections.display_label: survived the same Wyoming Massacre as a child

military_service.notes: 3rd Co., 1st Regt CT, May 1775, Wyoming Valley PA; marched under Gen. Schuyler to NY. 1776: 10th Continental Regt CT under Col. Parsons. 1777: commissioned Captain, 24th Regt CT (Westmoreland Regt). May 27, 1778: commissioned Surgeon. Surgeon to militia under Col. Dennison, Forty Fort 1778-1779. Participated in Battle of Wyoming. Congress 1836 special act: $2,400 to heirs for war services.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hooker" explicit tribute to ancestor Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Continental Army surgeon at Wyoming Massacre 1778
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Surgeon in the Continental Army — 3rd Company, 1st Regiment Connecticut — and was living in the Wyoming Valley when the July 3, 1778 massacre killed approximately 300 Patriots. His first wife Sarah had died three weeks earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Family Bible recorded wife's death before the massacre
narrative_blocks.body: His Oxford-printed Bible (1772) preserves the record of her death at 'about nine o'clock at night' on Friday, June 12, 1778. She was buried on his own land at Jacob's Plains two days later, three weeks before the Wyoming Massacre.

narrative_blocks.header: Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him
narrative_blocks.body: He and his son-in-law James Sutton founded an iron works in the Lackawanna Valley. Those who knew him called him 'old forge' — and the town of Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety in Tunkhannock, Pennsylvania
narrative_blocks.body: He died July 17, 1815 in Tunkhannock, Wyoming County, Pennsylvania, age 90. He had outlived his first wife by 37 years and his second wife as well.

narrative_blocks.header: Congress paid his heirs for his war service
narrative_blocks.body: Decades after his death, Congress passed a special act in the 1830s granting his heirs $2,400 in commutation for his Revolutionary service as the sole surgeon at the Wilkes-Barre garrison in the Wyoming Valley.

burial.plot_notes: Jackson Cemetery, Tunkhannock, Wyoming County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Miner's History of Wyoming.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains, Westchester County NY.
Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She followed her father to the Wyoming Valley
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Smith married James Sutton and removed with him and her father, Dr. William Hooker Smith, to the Wyoming Valley of Pennsylvania, settling at Exeter where Sutton's mills stood.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Daniel Sutton and Martha Goodall Sutton.

bio.bio_blurb: Wyoming Valley pioneer and mill owner

burial.plot_notes: Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: A creek in the Wyoming Valley bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: A merchant at North Castle, New York, James Sutton sold out at the Revolution's onset and removed to Exeter in the Wyoming Valley, building a saw and grist mill on a stream named for him, Sutton's Creek.

narrative_blocks.header: He guarded the women and children at Forty Fort
narrative_blocks.body: A Quaker scrupulous about bearing arms, Sutton was nonetheless stationed inside Forty Fort on the day of the 1778 Wyoming Massacre, charged with protecting the women, children, and aged who sheltered there.

cross_connections.display_label: was a militia surgeon at the 1778 Wyoming Massacre

narrative_blocks.header: A Wyoming Massacre survivor who reached ninety-six
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1773, Deborah Sutton was a small child at the Wyoming settlement during the 1778 raid and survived it, living on to the age of ninety-six in the Pennsylvania valley her family had helped settle.

burial.plot_notes: Delta Cemetery, Westernville, Oneida County NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son painted her portrait
narrative_blocks.body: Polly Sutton Catlin sat for a portrait by her son, the painter George Catlin; the picture is now held by the Smithsonian American Art Museum.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of fourteen, including a famous painter
narrative_blocks.body: Raised in the Wyoming Valley by pioneer settlers, Polly married the lawyer Putnam Catlin in 1789 and raised fourteen children — the fifth of whom was the artist George Catlin.

narrative_blocks.header: He drowned crossing the Susquehanna in 1842
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Sutton farmed, milled and lumbered the family homestead at Exeter until 1842, when the skiff he was crossing the Susquehanna in went down and he and a companion were lost.

burial.plot_notes: Forty Fort Cemetery, Forty Fort, Luzerne County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She died weeks before the Wyoming Massacre
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Smith Gustine died in June 1778, only weeks before the Battle of Wyoming swept the valley where her family had settled; she was buried at Forty Fort.

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Revolutionary surgeon who escaped the Wyoming Massacre

burial.plot_notes: Old Graveyard, Carlisle, Cumberland County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: He followed his father-in-law to Wyoming
narrative_blocks.body: Lemuel Gustine learned medicine from Dr. William Hooker Smith, married Smith's daughter Susannah, and removed with his father-in-law to the Wyoming Valley, serving alongside him as an assistant surgeon in the Continental army.

narrative_blocks.header: He escaped the Wyoming Massacre by the Susquehanna
narrative_blocks.body: Present at the surrender of Forty Fort, Gustine signed the capitulation and — having buried his wife only weeks earlier — fled the July 1778 massacre by floating down the Susquehanna with his infant daughter Sarah, reaching Carlisle.

narrative_blocks.header: She was called the last survivor of the Wyoming Massacre
narrative_blocks.body: As a three-year-old, Sarah Gustine escaped the 1778 Wyoming Massacre with her father down the Susquehanna; at her death in 1856 she was said to be the last living survivor of that day.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY; later church construction extended over his grave. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

notable.notable_blurb: Physician-minister at White Plains for thirty years

cross_connections.display_label: Young friend in New York who drew him toward the ministry

career.notes: Practiced medicine alongside ministry for nearly 30 years. 'Said to have been a very successful physician... remarkably successful in cases of insanity.'
Served the White Plains church which was destroyed in the Battle of White Plains October 1776, seven years after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked the Hudson with Jonathan Edwards as friend
narrative_blocks.body: He was a personal friend of Jonathan Edwards, the most important American theologian of the eighteenth century. On one occasion, walking together along the banks of the Hudson, Smith confided to Edwards: 'To be separated from my endeared friends and companions are the most bitter trials of my life.' The friendship spanned Smith's quarter-century at White Plains and Edwards's career through Northampton, Stockbridge, and Princeton.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Presbyterian minister for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine and ministry simultaneously at Rye and White Plains for nearly thirty years — a rare dual vocation in colonial America. Contemporaries described him as 'remarkably successful in cases of insanity.' His Yale class of 1727 trained him for ministry; the medicine he taught himself.

narrative_blocks.header: His church destroyed at Battle of White Plains
narrative_blocks.body: Seven years after his death in 1771, the Battle of White Plains was fought in front of his Presbyterian church on Oct 28, 1776 — and the building was destroyed. A second church on the same ground was later destroyed by fire. The third — built of gray stone — was eventually extended over his grave, and the original upright slab was moved further back.

narrative_blocks.header: A church was later built over his grave
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Dr. John Smith served nearly thirty years as Presbyterian minister at Rye and White Plains. After his death the White Plains meetinghouse was rebuilt over his burial spot, and his gravestone was moved back to accommodate it.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
